ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for mazesoba. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0340 (Pasta and noodles) is the closest available parent. No mazesoba-specific FoodEx2 code exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for mazesoba or brothless ramen as a specific food concept.

preparation_method: garnished with green onion and nori

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of mazesoba is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 200 g of cooked wheat noodles, 80 g of minced pork topping, and 120 g of sauce, egg yolk, and garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Ramen (noodle dish with toppings, composite estimate) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA or HPB entry exists for mazesoba with spicy chili sauce. Values are estimated by component assembly: cooked wheat noodles (USDA FDC #20121), minced pork (USDA FDC #10006), soy-sesame sauce, chili oil, and egg yolk, weighted to a typical mazesoba recipe ratio. Energy and macros are cross-checked against published Japanese brothless ramen nutritional analyses in peer-reviewed food science literature. Confidence is medium due to restaurant-specific variation.

gi_source.title: Noodles, wheat-based, cooked — Glycemic Index Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for mazesoba with spicy chili sauce. GI of 55 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked wheat noodles (GI range 47–58, mean ~55, medium category). The fat and protein content of the dish (pork, egg yolk, sesame oil) will moderate postprandial glucose response, so GI 55 is a conservative upper estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 90) / 100 = 49.5, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving ≈ 90 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~620 mg per 100 g; ~2480 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid mazesoba due to its very high sodium load from soy-based sauce and chili condiments, which can substantially exceed the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index wheat noodles with moderate carbohydrate load (~90 g per serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume mazesoba in controlled portions, as the medium-GI wheat noodles and high carbohydrate load per serving can cause a significant postprandial blood glucose rise; pairing with protein and fat toppings partially attenuates the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium, moderate saturated fat, and cholesterol from pork and egg yolk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat mazesoba as an occasional food due to its combination of high sodium (~2480 mg per serving), saturated fat from minced pork, and dietary cholesterol from egg yolk, all of which are risk factors for adverse cardiovascular outcomes.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mazesoba (まぜそば) is a Japanese brothless noodle dish, literally meaning 'mixed noodles', that originated in Nagoya and became widely popular across Japan and in Japanese ramen restaurants internationally. Unlike conventional ramen, mazesoba is served without soup broth; instead, thick wheat noodles are tossed directly with a rich, umami-forward sauce made from soy sauce, sesame oil, oyster sauce, and chili oil. The spicy chili sauce variant adds a pronounced heat from chili paste or chili oil, giving the dish a bold, fiery character. Standard toppings include seasoned minced pork (often cooked with garlic and ginger), a raw egg yolk placed in the centre, finely sliced green onions, nori (dried seaweed), bamboo shoots (menma), and sometimes dried bonito flakes or a dusting of fish powder. The diner mixes all components together before eating, creating a glossy, intensely flavoured coating on each noodle strand. The texture is chewy and satisfying, with the fat from the pork and egg yolk emulsifying into the sauce. Nutritionally, mazesoba is energy-dense, with significant carbohydrate from wheat noodles, moderate protein from pork and egg, and notable fat from sesame oil and chili oil. Sodium content is high due to soy-based sauces. Clinically, it is a concern for hypertension and cardiovascular disease due to sodium and saturated fat, and requires portion control for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes given its medium glycemic index and high carbohydrate load per serving. In Singapore, it is served at specialist Japanese ramen restaurants such as Ramen Matsuri.
common_user_queries: Is mazesoba the same as ramen?
What is the difference between mazesoba and tsukemen?
Where to eat mazesoba in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for mazesoba with spicy chili sauce exists in any primary approved source (HPB, USDA, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated by component assembly from USDA FDC SR Legacy entries for cooked wheat noodles and minced pork, weighted to a typical mazesoba recipe ratio, and cross-checked against published Japanese noodle dish analyses. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wheat noodles. Confidence is medium due to restaurant-specific variation in sauce composition, portion size, and topping ratios.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-assembled estimates; no single approved source entry covers mazesoba with spicy chili sauce as a composite dish. Values should be reviewed if a direct HPB or USDA Branded Food entry becomes available for this specific product. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for estimation. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for estimation. 4. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat are estimated from USDA FDC pork and sesame oil component data. 5. The input food_group 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' has been corrected to 'Grains and staples' as mazesoba is a noodle dish, not a condiment. 6. The input description 'Soup comes with all Mazesoba' appears to be a menu note about an accompanying soup side; mazesoba itself is a brothless dish. 7. Serving size of 400 g is a typical restaurant portion estimate; actual serving weight at Ramen Matsuri may vary. 8. Glycemic load per serving = (55 × 90) / 100 = 49.5, based on estimated 90 g carbohydrate per 400 g serving.
